title: The Undercover Economist
subtitle: Exposing Why the Rich Are Rich, the Poor Are Poor—and Why You Can Never Buy a Decent Used Car
author: Tim Harford
themes: Scarcity creates market power that explains who gets what in the economy
Price targeting allows businesses to extract maximum willingness to pay
Perfect competition creates an efficient 'world of truth'
Externalities cause market failures requiring targeted government intervention
Asymmetric information distorts markets and explains healthcare failures
Game theory illuminates strategic behavior in business and conflict
Comparative advantage makes globalization beneficial despite disruption
Institutions and property rights determine whether nations prosper or stagnate
Incentives drive all economic behavior — follow the incentives to understand outcomes
awards: FT Business Book of the Year Award shortlist (2006)
excerpt: When you buy a cappuccino at a train station for $4.50, you are not just paying for coffee beans, milk, and labor — you are paying for the privilege of buying coffee at a location where space is scarce and demand is high. The Undercover Economist reveals the hidden economic logic behind everyday transactions, from Starbucks pricing to traffic jams to why you can never find a decent used car.
